### Post by stinkeye on 2012-04-14
> **VinDSL said:**
> You're right!  Maybe I should revisit the calendar.

Took nine stabs to get the current one right.  LoL!  :D

Sometimes, I'm like a dog with a bone, and I won't let it go...

Hi Vin..,I have your 9th stab and it seems to work here to change to Monday start by just adding the **-M** switch.
eg
```
${execpi 60 VinDSL_Cal_9=`date +%-d`; ncal -M -C -h | sed -e 's/\r//g' -e 's/^/ /g' -e '1d' -e 's/\<'"$VinDSL_Cal_9"'\>/${color4}&${color3}/'}
```

---

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-04-18
Back on topic...

Occasionally, I've been contacted by someone having problem(s) with conky-all 1.8.1-6

EXAMPLE: conky-all 1.8.1-6 (left) || conky-all 1.8.1-5 (right)

[INDENT][INDENT][IMG]http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-17-apr-2012-conky-all-probs.png[/IMG][/INDENT][/INDENT]


It's happened enough times, that a pattern has started to develop.

Thus, I've added the following comment(s) to the .conkyrc script:

```
####
## Prerequisites (required)
#
#  conky-all 1.8.0 or [COLOR="Red"]**1.8.1-5**[/COLOR]
#  cURL - Command Line Tool
#  xsltproc - Command Line Tool
#  UTF-8 Compatible Text Editor
#
## Tips n' Tricks
## Several ppl (including myself) have experienced issues with conky-all 1.8.1-6
## In every instance, downgrading to conky-all 1.8.1-5 has solved the problem(s).
## I recommend downgrading (and pinning) conky-all 1.8.1-5 until things get sorted.
## conky-all 1.8.1-5 can be downloaded here: [https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/conky-all/1.8.1-5]("https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/conky-all/1.8.1-5")
```

If you're running conky-all 1.8.1-6, I suggest downgrading to 1.8.1-5, until further notice. ;)

---

I'm running conky-all 1.8.1-6 and the big problem is execi commands don't run 
until your uptime reaches the execi intervals. Outputs "(null)".
So on a fresh boot I get as in the pic until my uptime reaches 1800 secs
which corresponds to my execi intervals.
Pinning is probably best but everything else seems ok so I have left it for now, hoping for an update.

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-04-24
> **ngrieb said:**
> Wow, a bit harsh. 

Just tossing an idea out there to bring a visual aid to creating overlays like this and making them customizable to people who might not want to a) parse endless lines of code or, b) lack the proper programming background for it.

I was under the impression that this was supposed to be a collaborate community with the goal of making Ubuntu easier to use for the general public and furthering its development. Clearly I must be mistaken. I will take my ideas elsewhere.

FYI: The laziest thing that one can possibly do is copy and paste the code for use.

You clearly didn't read the first post of this HowTo.  We're not here to build a community, to make friends, to collaborate on how VinDSL does his scripts, or to share ideas.  We're here to teach people how to get VinDSL's Conky Setup running, since so many have asked how to do it.  His design is quite popular, and many people without a prior programming background have wanted to have it running.  The community and the rest of the forums are there for the rest.  In the process, a complete set of his code scripts are on display, and the HowTo is written to give the user as much background as they need to get it running, so that they don't require any sort of background knowledge beforehand.

Between VinDSL and myself we have several years worth of experience with the code, and getting new users up and running.  Specifically oriented around helping new users.  He wrote the code, I wrote the HowTo after so many requests for help.  We have a lot of experience in what it takes to get it all running, and have tried the do-it-for-me scripts out there.  None of them gets the new user up and running as fast as copying and pasting chunks of code and customising it to the user's needs.  It is the fastest, easiest, and most efficient way of teaching the user while getting it running.  The problem becomes the user runs the do-it-for-me script and then the script stops working due to one update or another to the system, and then you have to talk the user out of the script and into the code itself.  By the time you do that, you might as well have just taught them to code it themselves, and so we've skipped the do-it-for-me step and went straight to "You're perfectly capable of doing this. Here's How."

And, as VinDSL keeps reminding me, 28+ Thousand hits on the thread without issue can't be wrong.  It's obviously working this way.

But, by all means, feel free to start your own thread around your own script.  But, not here in this thread.  We are trying to keep things as simple and to the point as possible to make helping new users get started as easy as possible on everyone involved.  Have doubts?  Read the HowTo yourself in it's entirety instead of questioning the method.  If you genuinely find fault with it, let me know.  If not?  Don't waste my time with your laziness in not wanting to read and do things properly.  This method works for 28+ Thousand times versus your one moment of do-it-for-me laziness.

---

### Post by pressko on 2012-05-01
Hi, 

Awesome work !! (bow) to all of you  ! :) Although I have two simple questions... i hope. 

1. How can I make hr = horizontal line ( like this one in SYSYEM) shorter ? Because now it's longer than my background base. 

2. What's the difference between execi and execpi ? I'm asking which one is suitable for querying CPU temp, for an example and why ?  

10x in Advance !!! :guitar:

---

### Post by Sector11 on 2012-05-02
> **pressko said:**
> Hi, 

Awesome work !! (bow) to all of you  ! :) Although I have two simple questions... i hope. 

1. How can I make hr = horizontal line ( like this one in SYSYEM) shorter ? Because now it's longer than my background base.

Try:
```
${cpubar cpu0 1,50}
```

> **pressko said:**
> 2. What's the difference between execi and execpi ? I'm asking which one is suitable for querying CPU temp, for an example and why ?

[LIST=1]
[*]exec = **exec**ute a command in conky
[*]execi = adds an **i**nterval of x seconds: ${execi 10}
[*]execp = **p**arses the output of the command, so you can insert things like ${color red}hi!${color}
[/LIST]

exec**pi** - is a combination of exec**i** and exec**p**

If you want colour in the command you need the 'p' if you want to query the CPU less often than the "update_interval" you need execi at least.

${hwmon} however does not need an "exec" command

> **pressko said:**
> 10x in Advance !!! :guitar:

You're welcome.

---

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-05-07
> **Starks said:**
> Is there a script for installing everything so I can get right to tweaking to fit my system?

No.  Don't ask for such things.  It's nowhere near hard enough to warrant the need for an install script.  By the time you have it "Installed" for you, AKA "The Do-It-For-Me Method", you could also have copied the code you want and, reading ALL of this guide in the first post, fit it to your system yourself.  Also, if the Weather system changes, and it does, doing it yourself will mean you can do any updates without hassle.  If you were relying on a "Do-It-For-Me" method, you'd be stuck waiting for someone to make a new script.  Sometimes that person isn't around anymore.  Sometimes it's just a matter of changing one character here or there.

No, it's best if you just learn how to do the code yourself.  I personally wrote the first post so it would be easy to understand and follow along with.  That's what we put this thread here for.  We're not here to make friends, have conversations, or take requests.  We're just here to give you what you need to know in order to get it running for yourself.  If you can't be bothered to read the first post and try to learn, it's not our problem.

### Post by Raizor on 2012-05-10
First off, thanks to 42 and Vin for a great write up and great scripts.

I've read through them all, including all subsequent posts, but hadn't seen this specific question asked:

For the life of me, even if I change the HEX color values in the conky.rc script as indicated in the post, my colors remain the same.  In all posts and snapshots in the thread, I noticed all colors are the same for everyone.

I'm on 12.04 Unity 3d.  Have you seen this before and / or am I missing something?

---

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-05-10
> **Raizor said:**
> First off, thanks to 42 and Vin for a great write up and great scripts.

I've read through them all, including all subsequent posts, but hadn't seen this specific question asked:

For the life of me, even if I change the HEX color values in the conky.rc script as indicated in the post, my colors remain the same.  In all posts and snapshots in the thread, I noticed all colors are the same for everyone.

I'm on 12.04 Unity 3d.  Have you seen this before and / or am I missing something?

Restart Conky, and always remember to save your work.  Beyond that, make sure you're changing the hex values of the colours BEFORE the # sign at the end.  If you replace the name with a hex value of your choice, it will change every instance of that colour throughout the script.  Once you save the file of course.

To be most specific to change a colour, you change the thing in Red, not the thing in Blue.  The thing in Blue is for reference, hence the # before it.:

```

####
## My colors (suit yourself)
#
color0 [COLOR="Red"]White[/COLOR]            [COLOR="Blue"]#FFFFFF[/COLOR]
color1 [COLOR="Red"]Ivory[/COLOR]            [COLOR="Blue"]#FFFFF0[/COLOR]
color2 [COLOR="Red"]Ivory2[/COLOR]           [COLOR="Blue"]#EEEEE0[/COLOR]
color3 [COLOR="Red"]Ivory3[/COLOR]           [COLOR="Blue"]#CDCDC1[/COLOR]
color4 [COLOR="Red"]Tan1[/COLOR]             [COLOR="Blue"]#FFA54F[/COLOR]
color5 [COLOR="Red"]Tan2[/COLOR]             [COLOR="Blue"]#EE9A49[/COLOR]
color6 [COLOR="Red"]Gray[/COLOR]             [COLOR="Blue"]#7E7E7E[/COLOR]
color7 [COLOR="Red"]AntiqueWhite4[/COLOR]    [COLOR="Blue"]#8B8378[/COLOR]
color8 [COLOR="Red"]DimGray[/COLOR]          [COLOR="Blue"]#696969[/COLOR]
color9 [COLOR="Red"]Tomato[/COLOR]           [COLOR="Blue"]#FF6347[/COLOR]

```

You can, of course, remove the words and put the hex value in their places and it will work just the same.

And because you reminded me:

---

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-05-10
**_UPDATE:_** 05/10/2012:

Better explanation of using colours has been put in.  I have looked down VinDSL's code so many times I forget to clarify some things.  This is one of the little catches I needed to make, and haven't done so until now.  

Hex values are stated by VinDSL in a comment at the end of the table, but that comment means you don't change those hex values to change the colours.  In fact, that third "Column" is irrelevant.  It's just a reference.  You change the "Value" of the colour.  If you change the "Value" to a Hex Value instead of a preset name, you can now change the colours using Hex.  Preset names are defined elsewhere on the Internet and I'm not going to cover them.  They're a name given to a certain Hex Value, and agreed upon by many format programs.  Browsers and Conky alike, as well as others.

---

> **_UPDATE:_** 05/10/2012:

Hex values are stated by VinDSL in a comment at the end of the table, but that comment means you don't change those hex values to change the colours.  In fact, that third "Column" is irrelevant.  It's just a reference.  You change the "Value" of the colour.  If you change the "Value" to a Hex Value instead of a preset name, you can now change the colours using Hex.  Preset names are defined elsewhere on the Internet and I'm not going to cover them.  They're a name given to a certain Hex Value, and agreed upon by many format programs.  Browsers and Conky alike, as well as others.

That explains it, since I do tons of web stuff with colors, I only use the Hex colors all of which include the # in them when referenced in code.  It never dawned on me that the hex color was just a reference.  My bad, but thank you for the update and the explanation.

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-05-11
> **Raizor said:**
> That explains it, since I do tons of web stuff with colors, I only use the Hex colors all of which include the # in them when referenced in code.  It never dawned on me that the hex color was just a reference.[...]
Sorry, for being late to the party.  :)

I was in Central Arizona, for 3 days, without web access -- nothing but rattlers, lizards, and wood spiders. LoL!

As 42dorian explained, I mostly use named colors.  I never cared for hex (base-16), because it's hard to finger count.  I can count sexagesimal (base-60) on one hand.  Hexadecimal requires both hands, every knuckle, every finger tip, and so forth and so on -- it's essentially impossible.   Heh!

Anyway, I throw those "hex references" in there for ppl that cannot use named colors, for this-or-that reason.  ;)

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-05-12
Yes.  There are many, many, many guides to colours on the Internet.  Even I have my own cheat sheet going.

[Hex Values Cheat Sheet]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11013109#post11013109")

The idea is to say they're out there and not clutter up the thread with more than you need to know.  There's already a metric ton of links and information in this HowTo thread.  Trying to make it the only source for everything is counter to the goal of not being in competition with the [Big Conky Thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=281865").

The more clutter, the more difficult this guide is to use for newer users.  We're going for simple, direct efficiency.

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-05-20
***UPDATE:*** 05/20/2012

Someone reminded me to tell people to use Code Tags when asking for help in the Big Conky Thread.  Problem solved.  When asking for help in the Conky Thread, it makes it easier on all the pros to edit your code straight in the thread instead of having to download it, change it, and re-post it to you.  If you make it easier, you get more people willing to help you through the process.

### Post by TheYetiWakes on 2012-05-25
Just to say many thanks for the effort and time put into this.  Fairly new to linux and following this guide through I was pleased to see just how well it worked for me 'out of the box'.  Its got me upto speed on a lot of other general Ubuntu tasks and procedures too.  As you say if in doubt go back and read through everything.

One small query if I may that i'm just asking here as i'm not sure if its this script related?  In System section where it shows CPU information is the "freq_g cpu0" instruction the same process used for all CPUs?  Only I have a 2.60ghz Core 2 duo E7400 and my conky displays as 1.60ghz and then every once in a while will change to 2.6ghz and change back again? 

Just after a lead as to if its something in the conky, something up with Ubuntu (it shows ok under details), or if my CPU is likely on the fritz and I shall go off and seek further help in a different thread.

---

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-05-25
> **TheYetiWakes said:**
> Just to say many thanks for the effort and time put into this.  Fairly new to linux and following this guide through I was pleased to see just how well it worked for me 'out of the box'.  Its got me upto speed on a lot of other general Ubuntu tasks and procedures too.  As you say if in doubt go back and read through everything.

One small query if I may that i'm just asking here as i'm not sure if its this script related?  In System section where it shows CPU information is the "freq_g cpu0" instruction the same process used for all CPUs?  Only I have a 2.60ghz Core 2 duo E7400 and my conky displays as 1.60ghz and then every once in a while will change to 2.6ghz and change back again? 

Just after a lead as to if its something in the conky, something up with Ubuntu (it shows ok under details), or if my CPU is likely on the fritz and I shall go off and seek further help in a different thread.

I do cover that in the HowTo.  Go back and check out the CPU section where I talk about CPU Throttling.  It comes in many, many forms.  It does sound like your system is tweaking the speed depending on power saving modes.  This can be wrong, of course.  Since all you've said is the CPU model.  I do suggest you go get some help disabling CPU Throttling.  What your system will call that will differ.  But, in every case, it's a power saving feature.  Either your BIOS will be able to disable it, or your OS.  How to do that is for another thread though.

Updated the HowTo with regards to the Gnome-Keyring bug, as well as the problems with Conky 1.8.1-6.  I've been putting off doing this because, to be honest here, I have kind of wanted the problem to resolve itself with updates.  Since there hasn't been any, I figured it is about time to address it so the questions don't pop up anywhere.  I know from experience, if there isn't a central place that tells people what to do, the questions pop up and they just don't go away until someone puts a solution out.  And that slows everything down.

Thanks! 

42dorian's most excellent HOWTO is magical, isn't it?

It's rather unique -- I dare say -- The best Conky Tutorial on the web!

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-06-15
> **VinDSL said:**
> Thanks! 

42dorian's most excellent HOWTO is magical, isn't it?

My script(s) simply offer a working example of the principles he's trying to convey.

He's dissected my code like a well-designed laboratory experiment. LoL!  :D

It's rather unique -- I dare say -- The best Conky Tutorial on the web!

Let's be honest about this, shall we?  YOU, Vin, designed something that was overwhelmingly popular to people who don't want to program or design for themselves.  

These said people come in, especially from the Ubuntu world of "Linux made easy", see your work, and want to get it running.  When they copy and paste your work, the fundamental understanding of their own systems is lacking to such a degree that they BELIEVE it's broken because it doesn't look identical to your screenshot.

This is not helped by your bizarre font size choices (Half point sizes?  Decimal point sizes?  REALLY?) which, I might add, you've never explained to any degree.  Also hurting the cause, you insist on experimenting with things "Because it's there" rather than "Because it's efficient"... Not helpful.  And, third strike against the situation... You stopped servicing people's questions about getting it running.  That part, I understand, it's overwhelming.  

But, when every other post in the Conky Community thread is "I have a version of VinDSL's Conky... It's Broken... Fix it For Me." this is ALSO frustration incarnate.  It's hard to be a "Community" of Conky users, discussing and experimenting with the display and design aspects when all we do is get the same Conky running for everyone.  Since there's no real Tech Support for Conky, there's a million questions to be asked, and no one is even remotely targeting the user's problems with solutions in any organised way.

The truth here, and it's the whole truth, is that I was sick of answering these questions from new users so many times without being able to point them to anywhere that can genuinely HELP them.  To all of the rest of you reading this, VinDSL really is a nice guy with a really twisted sense of humour when you talk to him.  He's weird as hell, and brilliant at designing things.  I RESPECT the popularity of his code, and I LIKE him as a person.  His code itself, it's layout, and those little quirks (Decimal Point Fonts!  MY GOD WHAT GOOD ARE THEY?  THEY MAKE NO DIFFERENCE BETWEEN THEM AND THE NEXT WHOLE NUMBER UP!  AND USE A STANDARD WEATHER CODE SYSTEM ALREADY!  PURPOSELY BEING DIFFICULT!) bugs the living SNOT out of me.  I don't use his code myself, I've just been staring at it so much in the past year or two that SOMEBODY with some technical background had to save the Community from being inundated with MORE VinDSL questions.

So, there you go.  I like the guy, not the code, and got frustrated at obvious questions that were being asked without any proper answers coming up.  It's not some miracle, I'm not the salvation of all.  It's a necessary evil to get people over the hump of copying his code, and onto the path of changing it to suit their individual tastes.  It weeds out the people too lazy to design for themselves from the people who will come back to the Conky world with new designs and ideas for the rest of us.  There's still not a suitable Tech Support structure in place for the rest of Conky, and no one takes it seriously enough to start it.  But, I did take a part the most popular source for Conky and put it together so people can at least stop saying they know nothing and are afraid of everything.  Everything beyond that is in the realm of personal opinion and speculation.  

I did this to help people.  It doesn't cover some important things, and the Big Conky Thread is over 2000 pages long now.  Chances of being able to use it as a suitable reference for anything is long since dead.  And, as the metaphor goes, "In the Land of the Blind, the One-Eyed Man is King."  This thread is a direct application of understanding only the most popular setup in the Conky Community.  If it's considered "The Best source on the Internet", then it's only because there aren't any others written to be practical applications.  It doesn't cover some important things to the Conky world, so it is severely lacking in completeness.  I still catch hell from other Conky experts because I wrote this.  This has, by no means, been "Fun" to do.

---

42dorian, I must thank you for this tutorial! I began to use Conky because of this thread. I learned so much! Enough to make me comfortable to try it out on my own and do a little bit of editing myself. I personally don't use VinDSL's script (awesome script, by the way!), but your thread gave me an understanding of how Conky works and what it does, which is what I was looking for. I like to make things myself, and not just copy other people's creations so I do appreciate the attention to detail by both you and VinDSL. Your thread has enabled me to learn how to design and program my Conky for myself, which is precisely what I like to do! For that, I say **THANK YOU!** I am new to Ubuntu Linux and a newborn baby to Conky so I am still learning the ropes, but I give full credit to you in helping me getting started! I intend to learn a lot more about Conky and come up with a creation of my own that I will be happy with and maybe others will too. Keep up the great work! ***IT IS VERY MUCH APPRECIATED! ***

> 42dorian, I must thank you for this tutorial! I began to use Conky because of this thread. I learned so much! Enough to make me comfortable to try it out on my own and do a little bit of editing myself. I personally don't use VinDSL's script (awesome script, by the way!), but your thread gave me an understanding of how Conky works and what it does, which is what I was looking for. I like to make things myself, and not just copy other people's creations so I do appreciate the attention to detail by both you and VinDSL. Your thread has enabled me to learn how to design and program my Conky for myself, which is precisely what I like to do! For that, I say **THANK YOU!** I am new to Ubuntu Linux and a newborn baby to Conky so I am still learning the ropes, but I give full credit to you in helping me getting started! I intend to learn a lot more about Conky and come up with a creation of my own that I will be happy with and maybe others will too. Keep up the great work! ***IT IS VERY MUCH APPRECIATED! ***

Relax Shadius.  I'm aware the thread is "Helpful" to people.  It's written to be that, and I update/change things when people find problems with the text.  It's when people go a little overboard on what it does, as Vin is prone to do these days, I tend to set the record straight.  "It's the greatest help in the world!!" No... If that's true, it's because no one else has tried.  It doesn't mean it's not helpful, it just puts reality back into the mix.  I didn't resurrect anyone from the grave, and I didn't transmute Lead into Gold with the wave of a magic wand.

I have said repeatedly throughout this thread that too much praise of the thread itself doesn't help new users.  It just clutters the thread.  Every so often, Vin gets it into his head that I need an ego boost and becomes my cheer leader for a while.  Sure, there are times I've "Discussed" how frustrating it is dealing with the old Conky Pros with this thread hanging over my head, overshadowing everything I say around them.  I've wanted to just stop several times.  But there's no one else doing the tech support side of this.  So I tend to come back to update things of any importance.

Now can we please try to keep our over-enthusiasm out of this thread?  Vin's last update was December of 2011, and there have been several Ubuntu updates since then, as well as a few Conky upgrades.  As much as an annoyance as it will probably be for Vin and I both, we're probably due for an update to the text and the code.  Not that much has changed, all the examples are still valid.  But, the code says 11.04 and 11.10, and Conky 1.8.0 and 1.8.1, with some minor warnings about 1.8.1-6.  As much as it's kinda pointless, I think the text and the code need to sync up with the time we're working in now, and that will involve some headaches.  The fewer rants the better going forward.

---

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-06-16
> **Shadius said:**
> I was just showing some appreciation. Sorry, you feel that it was a rant of over enthusiasm.

No, it's fine.  I'm just trying to mitigate the off-topic stuff.  Anyone who reads the second paragraph of the first post of the thread sees why I do this every so often.  This isn't the place to express a lot of thanks for doing the thread.  For the same reason you don't write "Thanks for the book!" in the back of the Encyclopaedia at your local Library.  It's a reference thread, and any discussion not about "Issue X isn't addressed" or "Issue Y is up with version Z, Here's Solution A" doesn't actually belong here.  Anything else is clutter, and potentially frustration for any new user who is HAVING problem X and the first post is between updates.

It's not that we don't care about you all, Vin and I do care.  But sharing what you did, your experiences with Conky, "Thanks For This!" and all that off-topic stuff belongs in the Big Conky Thread.  They're the community, not this thread.  We never intended to take anything away from them, just filter some of the problems out to get that thread back to the Community part.

### Post by Dngrsone on 2012-06-28
Hello, 42dorian.

I have a recommendation and a question or two.

The recommendation to people who are going to use VinDSL's script and modify them is to forego the autostart script at least initially and just call up conky via a terminal window. Doing so will show you what errors may be cropping up so you can better fix your code. I don't know if conky generates an error log, but I know from past experience that an unmonitored log can create all kinds of havoc if you are running on limited hard drive space...

Also, width changes won't reflect until after you reboot Ubuntu, in my experience (12.04 with Unity).

Question:  I am somewhat confused about where to go to look for answers to questions pertaining to the weather scripts.  Since I am using VinDSL's script which is now based off Google Weather, and such.  Where is the best location to find tips on this, such how to optimise the script to handle both two- and three-digit forecasts?

> **Dngrsone said:**

I have a recommendation and a question or two.

The recommendation to people who are going to use VinDSL's script and modify them is to forego the autostart script at least initially and just call up conky via a terminal window. Doing so will show you what errors may be cropping up so you can better fix your code. I don't know if conky generates an error log, but I know from past experience that an unmonitored log can create all kinds of havoc if you are running on limited hard drive space...


Good idea to do, yes.  Good idea for this thread?  No.  Problems that could show up in a terminal are all covered in many little side notes throughout the first post right now.  Things I know about, I make notes on.  Such as the Conky Version and Gnome Keyring problems, or the Weather.com XOAP service being discontinued.  Anything drastically wrong after they've followed along, I point them to the Big Conky Thread.  We're not trying to take anything away from them, so we deliberately leave some of this stuff unanswered so that it pushes newer users back to the Conky thread.  If they actually read everything from top to bottom in the first post, they don't need terminal running while they work.

> **Dngrsone said:**
> 
Question:  I am somewhat confused about where to go to look for answers to questions pertaining to the weather scripts.  Since I am using VinDSL's script which is now based off Google Weather, and such.  Where is the best location to find tips on this, such how to optimise the script to handle both two- and three-digit forecasts?

Abandon it.  VinDSL probably hates when I say it this way, but I intentionally left no help links for the GoogleAPI weather script.  It's flawed, it's backwards, and VinDSL was only enamoured with it for a while because it was different than what others were using.  It's a massively backward script.  It was covered, briefly, in the old TBG Weather script thread, but it is pretty much dead now.  There is a new TBG Weather Script thread over at CrunchBang, and both of those links, old and new, are in my Sig.  Try one of those scripts.  It will be better for you in the long run.  I know they're not as simplified as the other ones, but they are the absolute best way to generate a genuinely personal weather section.

As I've said many times in the thread so far, the lazy way is actually the harder way.  Just because you don't "Feel" like placing every element, doesn't mean it's not easier that way.  Plus, there are shortcuts that help with how readable that section can be.  Again, we just don't cover them in THIS thread.  Weather is Teo's thing, Lua is mrpeachy's, and maintaining this HowTo is mine.  Or, in general, that's how it's sorted.

### Post by 42dorian on 2012-07-10
**_UPDATE (DELAYED):_** 06/10/2012

I do, indeed have an answer from VinDSL about the Decimal Fonts issue.  After years of having no answer, he has given one.  Unfortunately, due to some unknown reason, I am unable to edit the first post anymore.  I have a query into the Admins about it, and I'll let you know if anything develops.

In the mean time:  Onto the reason!

VinDSL has admitted that it is one of his quirks.  There is no TECHNICAL reason for the decimal fonts.  The way VinDSL does his designing is for his own personal tastes.  You have to admit, since so many people copy his design, he has a good eye for the aesthetics.  But, sometimes he's not happy with the whole numbers, and feels the need to use decimals in order to place them, for himself, in an exact place on the screen.  Where most of the rest of us will just use screenruler and put a font in a whole number, lining everything up to the closest pixel, VinDSL does not.  Yes, he uses Screenruler, but if screenruler isn't precise enough for his eyes, he adjusts the font sizes for the element he's working on, starting at increments of 0.11 until he is satisfied with how it looks to his eyes.

If you just read that, and thought it was weird, here's a better insight.  He designs for his eyes, not for the pixels.  The rest of us, wanting a more technically suitable setup, tend to design by the numbers, and only adjust those numbers when we run out of room in the Conky Window.  Where we would just make the window better, or move an element elsewhere, VinDSL forces the Fonts to comply with the aesthetics.

What does this mean?  Nothing.  Absolutely nothing.  It still means you have to design for your own needs, whether that be getting it to fit a different screen than his, or making room for what you want Conky to do, design for yourself.  The Decimal Fonts are not going to make or break the entire setup for you.  They are just for VinDSL's eyes.

*EDIT*  As Per [THIS]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1949027") This HowTo is currently suspending.

Since VinDSL's Conky is not a part of Ubuntu, moving it to a solely Ubuntu controlled WIKI defeats the purpose of this HowTo.  Some discussions will be had in the next little while regarding this.

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-08-05
[QUOTE=diabolicalangle]Hey buddy,

Saw your post on the how-to thread for your setup. Could you reply with your more recent code? That would be a lifesaver. I'm going to have to especially work on the weather section. Thanks man!

diabolical[/QUOTE]
Hi diabolicalangle,
I really haven't changed anything since December, except a little spacing, alignment, and colors, here n' there -- just minor things that everyone changes to suit their personal preferences.

I'm running the LXDE interface on top of Ubuntu 12.10 alpha 2 right now, which uses a different display DPI. 

```
vindsl@Zuul:~$ apt-cache policy lxde
lxde:
  Installed: 0.5.0-4ubuntu3
  Candidate: 0.5.0-4ubuntu3
  Version table:
 *** 0.5.0-4ubuntu3 0
        500 http://mirrors.se.eu.kernel.org/ubuntu/ quantal/universe i386 Packages
        100 /var/lib/dpkg/status
vindsl@Zuul:~$
```

Because of this, the Conky output looked squished.

Here's how LXDE looks, after the DPI "fix" (documented below)...


[INDENT][[IMG]http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-5-aug-2012.1(650x520).png[/IMG]]("http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-5-aug-2012.1.png")[/INDENT]


In order to save a lot of work, it's easier to change the DPI in my xorg config to 96x96 DPI.  That way, Conky looks fine. Plus, I can use the same .conkyrc script for LXDE/Openbox, Unity, GS, and Gnome 2 installs.

Here's my /etc/X11/xorg.conf settings (in case you're running LXDE, Lubuntu, E17, et cetera):

I added the code highlighted in **[COLOR="DarkRed"]RED[/COLOR]**, in the monitor section.

```
# nvidia-settings: X configuration file generated by nvidia-settings
# nvidia-settings:  version 304.32  (buildd@platinum)  Sat Aug  4 14:26:19 UTC 2012


Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Layout0"
    Screen      0  "Screen0" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Keyboard0" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Mouse0" "CorePointer"
    Option         "Xinerama" "0"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Mouse0"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "no"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Keyboard0"
    Driver         "kbd"
EndSection

**[COLOR="DarkRed"]Section "Monitor"[/COLOR]**

    # HorizSync source: edid, VertRefresh source: edid
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "DELL 1907FP"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 81.0
    VertRefresh     56.0 - 76.0
[B][COLOR="DarkRed"]    Option         "DPMS"
    Option         "DPI" "96 x 96"[/COLOR][/B]
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Device0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 7600 GT"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Device0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "Stereo" "0"
    Option         "nvidiaXineramaInfoOrder" "CRT-0"
    Option         "metamodes" "nvidia-auto-select +0+0"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```

My preferred media player is Guayadeque, these days...

```
##################################
##   RHYTHMBOX (Experimental)   ##
##################################
${if_running rhythmbox}
${voffset -2}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8}${color4}RHYTHMBOX${offset 8}${color6}${voffset -2}${hr 1}${font}
${voffset 2}${font DroidSans:size=8.25}${color3}${if_match "${execpi 2 expr length "`/usr/bin/rhythmbox-client --print-playing-format %tt | head -n 1`"}" >= "48"}${alignr 15}${scroll 38 4* ${execi 2 rhythmbox-client --print-playing-format %tt --no-start}}${font}${else}${alignc}${execi 2 rhythmbox-client --print-playing-format %tt --no-start}${font}${endif}${endif}
##################################
##    BANSHEE (Experimental)    ##
##################################
${if_running banshee}
${voffset -25}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8}${color4}BANSHEE${offset 8}${color6}${voffset -2}${hr 1}${font}
${voffset 8}${font DroidSans:size=8.25}${color3}${if_match "${execpi 2 expr length "`/usr/bin/banshee --query-title --no-present | cut -f1- -d " "`"}" >= "48"}${alignr 15}${scroll 38 4* ${execi 2 banshee --query-title --no-present | cut -f2- -d " "}}${font}${else}${alignc}${execi 2 banshee --query-title --no-present | cut -f2- -d " "}${font}${endif}${endif}
[B][COLOR="DarkRed"]##################################
##   GUAYADEQUE (Experimental)  ##
##################################
${if_running guayadeque}
${voffset -37}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8}${color4}GUAYADEQUE${offset 8}${color6}${voffset -2}${hr 1}${font}
${voffset 10}${font DroidSans:size=8.25}${color3}${if_match "${execpi 2 expr length "`qdbus org.mpris.guayadeque /\Player org.freedesktop.MediaPlayer.GetMetadata | grep title | cut -f2- -d" "`"}" >= "48"}${alignr 15}${scroll 38 4* ${execi 2 qdbus org.mpris.guayadeque /\Player org.freedesktop.MediaPlayer.GetMetadata | grep title | cut -f2- -d" "}}${font}${else}${alignc}${execi 2 qdbus org.mpris.guayadeque /\Player org.freedesktop.MediaPlayer.GetMetadata | grep title | cut -f2- -d" "}${font}${endif}${endif}[/COLOR][/B]
```

And, of course, Conky 1.9.0-2  :)

```
vindsl@Zuul:~$ apt-cache policy conky
conky:
  Installed: (none)
  Candidate: 1.9.0-2
  Version table:
     1.9.0-2 0
        500 http://mirrors.se.eu.kernel.org/ubuntu/ quantal/universe i386 Packages
     1.9.0-2~ppa1~precise1 0
        500 http://ppa.launchpad.net/vincent-c/conky/ubuntu/ precise/main i386 Packages
vindsl@Zuul:~$ 

```

Anyway, that's the only difference(s) between what I'm running, and the (formerly) "Perpetual Data Dump" (First page).

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-08-27
I've been experiencing random 403 errors with the Google Weather API, the past week or so.  Specifically, the Current Temp & Today's Forecast would randomly be missing from the XML results.  I originally thought this was a local problem, since I run dev releases, alphas, betas, rcs, and so forth exclusively, but...

It would now appear that the end of the Google Weather API has come and Google has stopped the service. 

I don't expect an announcement from Google, because they never officially supported the Weather API.

iGoogle (the intended recepient) isn't using the API either.  It appears that they have switched to Weather Underground for their feed.

[INDENT]EXAMPLE:  [http://igoogle.wunderground.com/cgi-bin/findweather/getForecast?query=scottsdale,az](http://igoogle.wunderground.com/cgi-bin/findweather/getForecast?query=scottsdale,az)[/INDENT]


I'm sure we'll hear more about this change, from the community, during the next few days.  A decision will be made on which path to take with the Conky script, once I'm sure the Google Weather API is dead and buried.

I suspect the best option will be to look for a different free Weather API, as 42dorian wisely suggested, some time ago.  :D

---

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-02
> **paramvir said:**
> one word - wow - this is the same pic as yesterday but in chrome and ... - fantastic :D
Heh!  You have a sharp eye for detail, my friend! I wanted to showcase the notifier. :D

BTW, I *think* I have the script nailed down. I'll monitor it for bugs, this week. 

Here it is in Gnome-Shell...


[INDENT][[IMG]http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-2-sep-2012-1(650x520).png[/IMG]]("http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-2-sep-2012-1.png")[/INDENT]


This is preliminary (not a final code release), but here is my Conkywx weather template...

```
##################################
## VinDSL | rev. 12-09-02 18:31 ##
##################################
##   CONKYWX WEATHER TEMPLATE   ##
##################################
${voffset 4}${goto 39}${font Weather:size=40.75}${color2}y${font}
${voffset -52}${font RadioSpace:size=36.5}${color3}${alignc}[PT]${font}
${voffset -33}${goto 204}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color4}Today${font}
${voffset 0}${goto 200}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color3}[D1T]${font}
${voffset -41}${goto 188}${font KRARound:size=39}${color3}I${font}
${voffset 4}${font Ubuntu:size=25.5}${color4}${alignc 4}[CT]${font}
${voffset 8}${goto 84}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.35}${color4}Humidity:${offset 3}${color3}[HM]${font}${voffset 12}${goto 84}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.35}${color4}Barometer:${offset 3}${color3}[BR]${font}${voffset 12}${goto 84}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.35}${color4}Bar${offset 2}Trend:${offset 3}${color3}[FCTP]${font}${voffset 12}${goto 84}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.35}${color4}Sunrise:${offset 3}${color3}[SR]${font}${voffset 12}${goto 84}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.35}${color4}Sunset:${offset 3}${color3}[SS]${font}
${voffset -63}${goto 16}${font ConkyWindNESW:size=42}${color3}[BF]${font}${voffset -34}${goto 198}${font MoonPhases:size=34.5}[MIF]${font}
${voffset 1}${goto 22}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8.45}${color4}[WS]${offset 3}mph${goto 202}[MP1]${font}
${voffset 9}${goto 22}${font DroidSansMono:bold:size=8.35}${color3}[D2D]${goto 84}[D3D]${goto 142}[D4D]${goto 200}[D5D]${font}
${voffset 1}${goto 22}${font ConkyWeather:size=32}${color3}[D2F]${goto 82}[D3F]${goto 140}[D4F]${goto 198}[D5F]${font}
${voffset 2}${goto 22}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.5}${color4}[D2T]${goto 82}[D3T]${goto 140}[D4T]${goto 198}[D5T]${font}
```

And, this is my Conky weather script...

```

${voffset 4}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.25}${color4}WEATHER${offset 8}${color6}${voffset -2}${hr 1}${font}
${execpi 900 conkywx -t "/home/vindsl/.conky/weather/vindsl-font-template" |
sed -e 's/Calm'/'0.0/g' |

sed -e 's/Heavy Freezing Fog'/'Fog/g' |
sed -e 's/Scattered Clouds'/'Clouds/g'
}${font}
```


As you can see from the screenie, I have a limited amount of space to work with, thus the liberal use of sed.  ;)

There are 120+ possible conditions in WU...

[INDENT]LINKAGE:  [http://www.wunderground.com/autobrandinfo/SampleRawFeed.html](http://www.wunderground.com/autobrandinfo/SampleRawFeed.html)
[/INDENT]

Some of them would fit, but I had to shorten the rest.  LoL!

### Post by paramvir on 2012-09-03
> **VinDSL said:**
> Heh!  You have a sharp eye for detail, my friend! I wanted to showcase the notifier. :D

BTW, I *think* I have the script nailed down. I'll monitor it for bugs, this week. 
Some of them would fit, but I had to shorten the rest.  LoL!

Ok so- oye - i did notice that notifier - n missed mentioning it lol - A BIG thanks is in order :D

I have a thing for cpu usage - ok so we can have short condition and long condition.

thanks for the link I was looking for that info but I made it such that if I was not able to figure it out - just pulled it out of the downloaded html ;)

it is so much easier to handle this in the code rather than in conky - makes me go ugggghhhhhhh :D

and rather than using brute force perhaps a better approach in the interim till i add all this in the code try:

```

| sed 's/Light //g;s/Heavy //g;s/Severe //g;s/Widespread //g;s/Pellet //g;s/Freezing //g;s/.*Thunderstorms.*/Thunderstorm/g;s/Scattered //g;s/Heavy //g;s/Patches of //g;s/Low Drifting //g;s/Calm/0.0/g;s/Small //g;s/Precipitation//g' }${font}

```you can add more conditions as you want while making single call to sed ;)
This code will handle all cases which you have mentioned except one "Light Dust" comes out as Dust - we can live with that I guess

what do you think???

---

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-03
> **paramvir said:**
> What do you think???
That's a great idea!

There were soooo many conditions, and so many opportunities for making typos, I took the easy path and parsed them on separate lines in Conky.

The downside of using "brute force" in Conky is, I had to bump-up my (Conky) cache considerably, from 640k -> 4096k. :)

BTW, I ran across a more recent "Phrase Glossary", a few minutes ago...

[INDENT]LINKAGE:  [http://www.wunderground.com/weather/api/d/docs?d=resources/phrase-glossary#current_condition_phrases](http://www.wunderground.com/weather/api/d/docs?d=resources/phrase-glossary#current_condition_phrases)[/INDENT]

Looks like the same conditions, but it's organized better, for easier reading...

---

### Post by paramvir on 2012-09-03
> **VinDSL said:**
> That's a great idea!

There were soooo many conditions, and so many opportunities for making typos, I took the easy path and parsed them on separate lines in Conky.

The downside of using "brute force" in Conky is, I had to bump-up my (Conky) cache considerably, from 640k -> 4096k. :)

BTW, I ran across a more recent "Phrase Glossary", a few minutes ago...[INDENT]LINKAGE:  [http://www.wunderground.com/weather/api/d/docs?d=resources/phrase-glossary#current_condition_phrases](http://www.wunderground.com/weather/api/d/docs?d=resources/phrase-glossary#current_condition_phrases)[/INDENT]Looks like the same conditions, but it's organized better, for easier reading...

Yes it does get tedious but it saves ram and file size and ultimately I think faster.

Edit: another plus is you don't need to bother what the condition is - if it has a word in ur sed line and the list gives us the word to look for - n it will be removed. Rain is rain - light or heavy u gonna get wet :D

Internally I am also abbreviating since it is not just day but night conditions to look at - which need to relate to the wx images and fonts. So the conditons text is a pass through. Very often they use tstorm or t-storm in place of thunderstorm - a lot of undocumented abbreviations.

The idea is no more sed-ding in conky - if we can help it :D

I have already put in a new feature which will let you know if a newer version is released in the notification popup and in the terminal as well. The check is once in 24 hours. :P

BTW I loooove your wall papers - just auwwsummm :D

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-03
> **paramvir said:**
> The idea is no more sed-ding in conky - if we can help it [...] :D

BTW I loooove your wall papers - just auwwsummm :D
Thanks!  The "Fantasy Fairy" is a keeper. I dump most walls after a few minutes/hours.  LoL!  :D

And, yes, you're right.  I love sed/grep/awk/cut, et cetera, but they have their place.  They're so much fun, it's easy to get carried away.  ;)

I had one of those "OMG!" moments, when I turned on the computer this morning.  After spending most of yesterday tweaking the weather section, it looked like garbage, to a fresh set of eyes.

The colors were too bright and contrasting -- the fonts were conflicting with each other -- the spacing was off -- I didn't like the "0.0 mph" thing -- blah, blah, blah.  This is all normal.  It's the reason Conky is so addictive.  I'll probably be tweaking the weather section for months.

Anyway, I spent a couple of hours making lots of subtle changes, and I'm much happier with the look n' feel now.

Here it is in Unity...


[INDENT][[IMG]http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-3-sep-2012-1(650x520).png[/IMG]]("http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-3-sep-2012-1.png")[/INDENT]


Here is the Conkywx weather template...

```
##################################
## VinDSL | rev. 12-09-03 11:05 ##
##################################
##   CONKYWX WEATHER TEMPLATE   ##
##################################
${voffset 4}${goto 39}${font Weather:size=44}${color6}y${font}
${voffset -62}${font RadioSpace:size=36}${color6}${alignc}[PT]${font}
${voffset 0}${goto 88}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Trend:${offset 3}${color6}[FCTT]${font}
${voffset -43}${goto 204}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Today${font}
${voffset 0}${goto 200}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color6}[D1T]${font}
${voffset -41}${goto 188}${font KRARound:size=39}${color3}I${font}
${voffset 8}${font Ubuntu:size=25}${color5}${alignc}[CT]${font}
${voffset 10}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Humidity:${offset 3}${color6}[HM]${font}${voffset 12}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Barometer:${offset 3}${color6}[BR]${font}${voffset 12}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Bar${offset 2}Trend:${offset 3}${color6}[FCTP]${font}${voffset 12}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Sunrise:${offset 3}${color6}[SR]${font}${voffset 12}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Sunset:${offset 3}${color6}[SS]${font}
${voffset -63}${goto 16}${font ConkyWindNESW:size=42}${color6}[BF]${font}${voffset -38}${voffset 2}${goto 195}${font MoonPhases:size=34.5}${color6}[MIF]${font}
${voffset 2}${goto 22}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8.45}${color3}[WS] mph${voffset -3}${goto 202}[MP1]${font}
${voffset 9}${goto 26}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.5}${color5}[D2D]${goto 86}[D3D]${goto 144}[D4D]${goto 204}[D5D]${font}
${voffset 1}${goto 22}${font ConkyWeather:size=32}${color6}[D2F]${goto 82}[D3F]${goto 140}[D4F]${goto 198}[D5F]${font}
${voffset 2}${goto 23}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.5}${color3}[D2T]${goto 83}[D3T]${goto 141}[D4T]${goto 199}[D5T]${font}

### Post by Rbbt on 2012-09-03
@VinDSL is it possible to share your conky folder?

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-03
> **Rbbt said:**
> @VinDSL is it possible to share your conky folder?
Sure!  Give me a while to cleanup my files, and I'll do a data dump this week.

If you're interested in Conkywx, you might go ahead and install it. 

Paramvir has included a lot of nice (example) scripts in the package. It'll take you time to config Conkywx, and find a reliable reporting station.

Once you get a working install, the rest is a piece-of-cake.  ;)

**EDIT**

BTW, the Conkywx deb "depends" on "conky" being installed.  Everyone I know runs "conky-all".

So, if you're running "conky-all", don't be surprised if you have to install "conky" too, to satisfy the Conkywx deb.

---

### Post by sparker256 on 2012-09-03
Been following your work for some time and have enjoyed what my desktop look like. I have installed your newest templete and script and have most working but think I have found a issue with conkywx as my screenshot will show.

It looks like conkywx thinks the forcast tempatures are in Celcius and converts them to Farenheit even though they already are in Farenheit. I have looked at the debug log and that is where I first saw what was going on.

I just wanted to say thanks for all your efforts.

Bill

---

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-03
Hi sparker256,

I *think* that's a bug too, but I didn't want to bother Paramvir with it, at this juncture.

I got the "conkywx.conf" file working by:

[LIST][*]NOT using XML data.[/LIST]
[INDENT]```
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# use_xml
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use XML data from wunderground - 1 for yes - 0 for no
# if this is set to 1 you will need the xml_ID for a station of your choosing
# 
**[COLOR="DarkRed"]use_xml=0[/COLOR]**
```[/INDENT]
[LIST][*]Adding "Units_downloaded" to the mix.[/LIST]
[INDENT]```
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Units to use and Downloaded
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
#  enter the units to get centigrade or farenheit 
#+ metric [SI] or imperial/english [IM] some call it Option= SI or IM
Units_to_Use=IM
**[COLOR="DarkRed"]Units_downloaded=IM[/COLOR]**
```[/INDENT]
I also had to try several reporting stations, until I found one that provided all the data I needed, but that's to be expected when you're dealing with Private Weather Stations (PWS).  ;)

---

### Post by sparker256 on 2012-09-03
Following your lead I have success. The other helpful piece of info was increasing the cache size.

Thanks again Bill

---

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-03
> **sparker256 said:**
> Following your lead I have success. The other helpful piece of info was increasing the cache size.[...]
Great! :D  

Glad you saw the comments about increasing the size from 640 -> 4096.

I should post that info here, so everyone sees it...

```
####
## This buffer is used for single lines, text, output from $exec, and other variables.
## Increasing the 'text_buffer_size' (too high) will drastically reduce Conky's performance.
## **[COLOR="DarkRed"]Decreasing the buffer size (too low) will truncate content and cause strange display output.[/COLOR]**
## Standard text buffer size is 256 bytes (cannot be less). Adjust YOUR buffer wisely!
## Tips n' Tricks from dk75:"You don't need to create a 12KiB buffer for every Conky config line."
#
**[COLOR="DarkRed"]text_buffer_size 4096[/COLOR]**
```

Some ppl may require an even bigger number, depending on what they've added to the script.

---

### Post by paramvir on 2012-09-04
> **VinDSL said:**
> Hi sparker256,

I *think* that's a bug too, but I didn't want to bother Paramvir with it, at this juncture.

I got the "conkywx.conf" file working by:

[LIST]
[*]NOT using XML data.
[/LIST]
[INDENT]```
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# use_xml
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use XML data from wunderground - 1 for yes - 0 for no
# if this is set to 1 you will need the xml_ID for a station of your choosing
# 
**[COLOR=DarkRed]use_xml=0[/COLOR]**
```[/INDENT]
[LIST]
[*]Adding "Units_downloaded" to the mix.
[/LIST]
[INDENT]```
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Units to use and Downloaded
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
#  enter the units to get centigrade or farenheit 
#+ metric [SI] or imperial/english [IM] some call it Option= SI or IM
Units_to_Use=IM
**[COLOR=DarkRed]Units_downloaded=IM[/COLOR]**
```[/INDENT]I also had to try several reporting stations, until I found one that provided all the data I needed, but that's to be expected when you're dealing with Private Weather Stations (PWS).  ;)

OK re the **[COLOR=DarkRed]Units_downloaded=IM [/COLOR]**- in my earlier builds I had made that part of the config but I got feedback that units downloaded were infact always centigrade so I took it out - though the code will still handle it.

So this is interesting - since no matter what I try I always get downloaded data in centigrade.

no worries we can have that option back in the next release ;)

Using xml data - the data comes from private weather stations feeding WU which has an update interval of about 2.5 seconds - and that is why this is there - but if your primary station is on rapid fire then it is fine to not use xml data.

and which other bugs insects have you found as u mention I *think* that's a bug **too**??? :D

---

### Post by paramvir on 2012-09-04
> **sparker256 said:**

### Post by paramvir on 2012-09-04
> **Rbbt said:**
> Yesterday I tried conkywx but most probably I won't use it because it makes conky long and my pc screen resolution is low. :icon_frown:

conkywx gets the weather data to conky - how you set your template makes the difference - how much information you show depends on your choice.

have a look at the slim template it shows slightly less info. you can also use the # character to comment out lines you do not want to see.

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-05
Tighten up the code a bit.  The "biggest" change is, I added an overlay to the moon phase icon.

```
##################################
## VinDSL | rev. 12-09-05 17:57 ##
##################################
##   CONKYWX WEATHER TEMPLATE   ##
##################################
${voffset 6}${goto 36}${font Weather:size=42.65}${color6}y${font}
${voffset -58}${font RadioSpace:size=32}${color6}${alignc}[PT]${font}
${voffset 2}${goto 88}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Trend:${offset 3}${color6}[FCTT]${font}
${voffset -43}${goto 204}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Today${font}
${voffset 0}${goto 200}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color6}[D1T]${font}
${voffset -41}${goto 188}${font KRARound:size=39}${color3}I${font}
${voffset 8}${font Ubuntu:size=24.5}${color5}${alignc}[CT]${font}
${voffset 8}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Humidity:${offset 3}${color6}[HM]${font}${voffset 11}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Barometer:${offset 3}${color6}[BR]${font}${voffset 11}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Bar${offset 2}Trend:${offset 3}${color6}[FCTP]${font}${voffset 11}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Sunrise:${offset 3}${color6}[SR]${font}${voffset 11}${goto 84}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color5}Sunset:${offset 3}${color6}[SS]${font}
**[COLOR="DarkRed"]${voffset -62}${goto 16}${font ConkyWindNESW:size=42}${color6}[BF]${font}${voffset -38}${voffset 2}${goto 195}${font MoonPhases:size=34.5}${color6}[MIF]${font}${voffset -35}${goto 197}${font MoonPhases:size=34.5}${color8}8${font}[/COLOR]**
${voffset 1}${goto 22}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8.45}${color3}[WS] mph${voffset -1}${goto 200}[MP1]${font}
${voffset 8}${goto 26}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.5}${color5}[D2D]${goto 86}[D3D]${goto 144}[D4D]${goto 204}[D5D]${font}
${voffset 1}${goto 22}${font ConkyWeather:size=32}${color6}[D2F]${goto 82}[D3F]${goto 140}[D4F]${goto 198}[D5F]${font}
${voffset 2}${goto 23}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.5}${color3}[D2T]${goto 83}[D3T]${goto 141}[D4T]${goto 199}[D5T]${font}
```

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-10
Here's a screenie, from this morning...

[INDENT][[IMG]http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-10-sep-2012-1(650x520).png[/IMG]]("http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-10-sep-2012-1.png")[/INDENT]


42dorian was right!  Using the Wunderground data feed (and Conkywx) has added a world of possibilities.

As you can see (or not) I've crammed a lot of functionality into a small space.  Hope I'm not getting too carried away.  :D

Here are the pertinent sections from my ".conkyrc"...
```
##################################
## VinDSL | rev. 12-09-10 11:01 ##
##################################
##    September 2012 Series     ##
##################################


<SNIP>


####
## This buffer is used for single lines, text, output from $exec, and other variables.
## Increasing the 'text_buffer_size' (too high) will drastically reduce Conky's performance.
## Decreasing the buffer size (too low) will truncate content and cause strange display output.
## Standard text buffer size is 256 bytes (cannot be less). Adjust YOUR buffer wisely!
## Tips n' Tricks from dk75:"You don't need to create a 12KiB buffer for every Conky config line."
#
text_buffer_size 4352


<SNIP>


##################################
##    CONKYWX WEATHER SCRIPT    ##
##################################
${voffset 4}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.25}${color4}CONKYWX${offset 8}${color6}${voffset -2}${hr 1}${font}
${execpi 900 conkywx -c "/home/vindsl/.conky/weather/conkywx.conf" -t "/home/vindsl/.conky/weather/vindsl-weather-template"|
####
## Windspeed [WS]
sed -e 's/Calm mph'/'\$\{offset 8\}Calm/g'|
####
## Moon Phases [MP]
sed -e 's/New Moon'/'\$\{offset -10\}New Moon/g'|
sed -e 's/Waxing Crescent'/'\$\{offset -3\}Waxing/g'|
sed -e 's/First Quarter'/'\$\{offset -3\}First Qtr/g'|
sed -e 's/Waxing Gibbous'/'\$\{offset -3\}Waxing/g'|
sed -e 's/Full Moon'/'\$\{offset -8\}Full Moon/g'|
sed -e 's/Waning Gibbous'/'\$\{offset -3\}Waning/g'|
sed -e 's/Last Quarter'/'\$\{offset -3\}Last Qtr/g'|
sed -e 's/Waning Crescent'/'\$\{offset -3\}Waning/g'|
####
## Current Conditions [CT]
sed -e 's/Light Snow Grains'/'Snow Grains/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Ice Crystals'/'Ice Crystals/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Ice Pellets'/'Ice Pellets/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Volcanic Ash'/'Volcanic Ash/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Widespread Dust'/'Light Dust/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Dust Whirls'/'Dust Whirls/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Low Drifting Snow'/'Drifting Snow/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Low Drifting Widespread Dust'/'Drifting Dust/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Low Drifting Sand'/'Drifting Sand/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Blowing Snow'/'Blowing Snow/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Blowing Widespread Dust'/'Blowing Dust/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Blowing Sand'/'Blowing Sand/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Rain Showers'/'Rain Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Snow Showers'/'Snow Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Ice Pellet Showers'/'Ice Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Hail Showers'/'Hail Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Small Hail Showers'/'Hail Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Thunderstorm'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Thunderstorms and Rain'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Thunderstorms and Snow'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Thunderstorms and Ice Pellets'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Thunderstorms with Hail'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Thunderstorms with Small Hail'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Freezing Drizzle'/'Freezing Drizzle/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Freezing Rain'/'Freezing Rain/g'|
sed -e 's/Light Freezing Fog'/'Freezing Fog/g'|
sed -e 's/Unknown Precipitation'/'Unknown/g'|
sed -e 's/Low Drifting Snow'/'Drifting Snow/g'|
sed -e 's/Low Drifting Widespread Dust'/'Drifting Dust/g'|
sed -e 's/Low Drifting Sand'/'Drifting Sand/g'|
sed -e 's/Blowing Widespread Dust'/'Blowing Dust/g'|
sed -e 's/Ice Pellet Showers'/'Ice Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Small Hail Showers'/'Hail Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Thunderstorms and Rain'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Thunderstorms and Snow'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Thunderstorms and Ice Pellets'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Thunderstorms with Hail'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Thunderstorms with Small Hail'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Snow Grains'/'Snow Grains/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Ice Crystals'/'Ice Crystals/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Ice Pellets'/'Ice Pellets/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Volcanic Ash'/'Volcanic Ash/g'|

sed -e 's/Heavy Dust Whirls'/'Dust Whirls/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Fog Patches'/'Fog Patches/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Low Drifting Snow'/'Drifting Snow/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Low Drifting Widespread Dust'/'Drifting Dust/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Low Drifting Sand'/'Drifting Sand/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Blowing Snow'/'Blowing Snow/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Blowing Widespread Dust'/'Blowing Dust/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Blowing Sand'/'Blowing Sand/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Rain Showers'/'Rain Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Snow Showers'/'Snow Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Ice Pellet Showers'/'Ice Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Hail Showers'/'Hail Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Small Hail Showers'/'Hail Showers/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Thunderstorm'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Thunderstorms and Rain'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Thunderstorms and Snow'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Thunderstorms with Ice Pellets'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Thunderstorms with Hail'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Thunderstorms with Small Hail'/'Thunderstorms/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Freezing Drizzle'/'Freezing Drizzle/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Freezing Rain'/'Freezing Rain/g'|
sed -e 's/Heavy Freezing Fog'/'Freezing Fog/g'
}${font}
##################################
##     CONKYWX TIME SCRIPT      ##
##################################
${voffset 6}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8}${color4}TIME${offset 8}${color6}${voffset -2}${hr 1}${font}
${execpi 900 conkywx -c "/home/vindsl/.conky/weather/conkywx.conf" -t "/home/vindsl/.conky/weather/vindsl-time-template"}${font}


<SNIP>
```


Please note:  I changed the name of the weather template, and added a second time template.

Here's the "vindsl-weather-template"...
```
##################################
## VinDSL | rev. 12-09-10 10:52 ##
##################################
##   CONKYWX WEATHER TEMPLATE   ##
##################################
${voffset 6}${goto 36}${font Weather:size=42.65}${color6}y${font}
${voffset -58}${font RadioSpace:size=32.2}${color6}${alignc}[PT]${font}
${voffset 2}${goto 83}${font}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}CoR:${offset 3}${color6}[D1P]${offset 2}/${offset 2}[FCTT]${font}
${voffset -43}${goto 204}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Today${font}
${voffset 0}${goto 200}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8}${color6}[D1T]${font}
${voffset -41}${goto 188}${font KRARound:size=39}${color3}I${font}
${voffset 7}${font Ubuntu:size=22.75}${color5}${alignc}[CT]${font}
${voffset 8}${goto 83}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Humidity:${offset 3}${color6}[HM]${font}${voffset 11}${goto 83}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Barometer:${offset 3}${color6}[BR]${font}${voffset 11}${goto 83}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Bar${offset 2}Trend:${offset 3}${color6}[FCTP]${font}${voffset 11}${goto 83}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Moonrise:${offset 3}${color6}[MR]${font}${voffset 11}${goto 83}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Moonset:${offset 3}${color6}[MS]${font}
${voffset -61}${goto 16}${font ConkyWindNESW:size=41.6}${color6}[BF]${font}${voffset -36}${goto 196}${font MoonPhases:size=35}${color6}[MIF]${font}${voffset -36}${goto 198}${font MoonPhases:size=35}${color8}8${font}
${voffset -1}${goto 22}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=8.45}${color3}[WS] mph${voffset -1}${goto 200}[MP]${font}
${voffset 9}${goto 23}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.5}${color5}[D2D]${goto 83}[D3D]${goto 141}[D4D]${goto 199}[D5D]${font}
${voffset 1}${goto 22}${font ConkyWeather:size=32}${color6}[D2F]${goto 82}[D3F]${goto 140}[D4F]${goto 198}[D5F]${font}
${voffset 1}${goto 23}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.5}${color3}[D2T]${goto 83}[D3T]${goto 141}[D4T]${goto 199}[D5T]${font}
```


And, the "vindsl-time-template"...
```
##################################
## VinDSL | rev. 12-09-09 18:35 ##
##################################
##    CONKYWX TIME TEMPLATE     ##
##################################
${voffset -5}${font RadioSpace:size=32}${color3}${if_match ${time %l}<=9}${alignc 7}${time %l:%M%p}${else}${if_match ${time %l}>=10}${alignc -1}${time %l:%M%p}${endif}${endif}${font}
${voffset 2}${alignc}${font DroidSansFallback:bold:size=7.55}${color5}Sunrise:${offset 3}${color6}[SR]${offset 3}|${offset 3}${color5}Sunset:${offset 3}${color6}[SS]${font}
```

That's about it, for now.  :)

I'll do a complete data dump, when the changes are finalized.

### Post by GreatDanton on 2012-09-10
@Rbbt then you can be happy, since your computer behave much faster than his. I suppose he ran out of ram, and therefore he need to use swap (VinDsl correct me if I am wrong :D. ### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-10
Oh, that swap...  

I find that my 1GB RAM-powered PC performs better if I tweak its "swappiness", so-called.

Here are my current "/etc/sysctl.conf" settings...

```
vindsl@Zuul:~$ cat /etc/sysctl.conf
#
# /etc/sysctl.conf - Configuration file for setting system variables
# See /etc/sysctl.d/ for additional system variables
# See sysctl.conf (5) for information.
#

#kernel.domainname = example.com

# Uncomment the following to stop low-level messages on console
#kernel.printk = 3 4 1 3

##############################################################3
# Functions previously found in netbase
#

# Uncomment the next two lines to enable Spoof protection (reverse-path filter)
# Turn on Source Address Verification in all interfaces to
# prevent some spoofing attacks
#net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter=1
#net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter=1

# Uncomment the next line to enable TCP/IP SYN cookies
# See http://lwn.net/Articles/277146/
# Note: This may impact IPv6 TCP sessions too
#net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1

# Uncomment the next line to enable packet forwarding for IPv4
#net.ipv4.ip_forward=1

# Uncomment the next line to enable packet forwarding for IPv6
#  Enabling this option disables Stateless Address Autoconfiguration
#  based on Router Advertisements for this host
#net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=1


###################################################################
# Additional settings - these settings can improve the network
# security of the host and prevent against some network attacks
# including spoofing attacks and man in the middle attacks through
# redirection. Some network environments, however, require that these
# settings are disabled so review and enable them as needed.
#
# Do not accept ICMP redirects (prevent MITM attacks)
#net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
#net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
# _or_
# Accept ICMP redirects only for gateways listed in our default
# gateway list (enabled by default)
# net.ipv4.conf.all.secure_redirects = 1
#
# Do not send ICMP redirects (we are not a router)
#net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0
#
# Do not accept IP source route packets (we are not a router)
#net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route = 0
#net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_source_route = 0
#
# Log Martian Packets
#net.ipv4.conf.all.log_martians = 1
#
# For Avast!
kernel.shmmax=100000000
#
[B][COLOR="DarkRed"]# Cannot leave things alone
vm.overcommit_memory = 1
vm.overcommit_ratio = 95
vm.dirty_ratio = 5
vm.swappiness = 99
[/COLOR][/B]#
vindsl@Zuul:~$ 

```

"99" makes heavy usage of the swap partition.

Here are Tomboy Notes, if you're interested in this sort of stuff...

> [SIZE="+1"]_Swappiness_[/SIZE]

What is swappiness and how do I change it?
The swappiness parameter controls the tendency of the kernel to move processes out of physical memory and onto the swap disk. Because disks are much slower than RAM, this can lead to slower response times for system and applications if processes are too aggressively moved out of memory.

swappiness can have a value of between 0 and 100
swappiness=0 tells the kernel to avoid swapping processes out of physical memory for as long as possible
**[COLOR="DarkRed"]swappiness=100 tells the kernel to aggressively swap processes out of physical memory and move them to swap cache[/COLOR]**
The default setting in Ubuntu is swappiness=60. Reducing the default value of swappiness will probably improve overall performance for a typical Ubuntu desktop installation. A value of swappiness=10 is recommended, but feel free to experiment. Note: Ubuntu server installations have different performance requirements to desktop systems, and the default value of 60 is likely more suitable.

Check swappiness:
cat /proc/sys/vm/swappiness

Change swappiness temporarily:
sudo sysctl vm.swappiness=10

Permanent change.  Edit:
sudo gedit ../../etc/sysctl.conf

Then add to bottom:
vm.swappiness=10

Save & reboot

Back on topic.  Don't want to clog this thread with minutia...  ;)

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-12
Cheers, Paramvir!  It's working great!  :)

I've been trying to leave your code alone, until you published an update...

For my templates, I turned the degree symbols back on, in the forecast temps:

```
convert_FC_units ()
{
	# change "23 | 21" temp format to "23/21" for day forecast
	infc1="$1"
	infc2=${infc1//[ ]/}   	# get rid of ALL spaces
	infcT=${infc2/\|*}		# get value left of |
	infcL=${infc2#*\|}		# get value right of |

	case $Units_to_Use in
	IM|MI)
		# 3rd option "1" is no Deg symbol "2" is with symble, 
		# remove 3rd option to get deg with F or C
		outfcT="$( convert_units "$infcT" **[COLOR="DarkRed"]"temp" 2[/COLOR]** )"
		outfcL="$( convert_units "$infcL" **[COLOR="DarkRed"]"temp" 2[/COLOR]** )"
		echo "$outfcT/$outfcL"
		;;
	SI|IS)
		# 3rd option "1" is no Deg symbol "2" is with symble, 
		# remove 3rd option to get deg with F or C
		outfcT="$( convert_units "$infcT" **[COLOR="DarkRed"]"temp" 2[/COLOR]** )"
		outfcL="$( convert_units "$infcL" **[COLOR="DarkRed"]"temp" 2[/COLOR]** )"
		echo "$outfcT/$outfcL"
		;;
	# *)
		# echo "$infcT/$infcL"
		# ;;
	esac
}
```
... and, got rid of the leading "0" in the 12-hour time displays.

```
converttohours ()
{
	local orgtime1=$( trim "$1" )
	check_if_time="${orgtime1:0:2}"
	if [[ $check_if_time =~ ^[0-9|:][:0-9]*$ ]]; then
		corrtime="${orgtime1:0:8}"
		
		if [[ $Time_format == 24 ]]; then
			if [[ "$orgtime1" == *SCT* ]]; then
				echo $( date --date "$corrtime 1 hour 30 min" +%R)
			else
				echo $( date --date="$corrtime" +%R )
			fi
		else
			if [[ "$orgtime1" == *SCT* ]]; then
				echo $( date --date **[COLOR="DarkRed"]"$corrtime 1 hour 30 min" "+%l:%M %p"[/COLOR]**)
			else
				# echo $( date --date="$corrtime" +%R )
				echo "$( date --date=**[COLOR="DarkRed"]"$corrtime" "+%l:%M %p"[/COLOR]** )"
			fi
		fi
	else
		echo "N/A"
	fi
}
```

Keep up the good work.  You're doing a fantastic job!  ;)

Hi Bill,
I have "commented-out" all SED calls, expect for Moon Phases, and the "Calm mph" wind speed thingie.  Incidentally, this allowed me to knock the "text_buffer_size" down to "2560" (with some fudge-room built in), as Paramvir predicted.

On the CoR:  I've been anguishing over this for days.

Personally, CoR means nothing to me, because I live in one of the hottest, driest places on Earth.  But, I *thought* other Conkystidors might care about it.  I would be leaning toward adding CoR values on one day, and leaning against it the next. LoL! :)

I read your post, this morning, but didn't have time to reply.

I mentioned "the CoR situation" to a co-worker today, and he said, "All I care about is the chance of rain."

So, between the two of you, it convinced me of the importance of CoR.  Thus, I added it to all forecasts...  ;)

### Post by VinDSL on 2012-09-14
> **paramvir said:**

Hi Paramvir,
Fantastic job, on your latest Conkywx update!

I kept all your SED calls, expect for "Scattered" & "Calm mph".

The "Calm mph" sed is actually running against wind speed, not current conditions.  And, if I remember correctly, I added the "mph" text to my Conky script, so I could truncate it, thus sed has to be run against my script, not at a core level.

I decided I preferred the word "Scattered" to any other word, or no word at all -- and adjusted the font size, so there was room for it. "Scattered" is what it is.  There really isn't any other word that takes its place, you know?  I judge that's why the weather reports use it. LoL!  :D

I really appreciate the accommodations you have made.  And, I'm sure others do, as well.  Conkywx is coming along very nicely.

Once, again, good job!  ;)

Sean - Many thanks for the kind words.

It was reported that a proxy server was blocking ping from working so the work around is using wget to the same job - in fact it works faster. For downloading data the tool used is wget. Normal rules should apply for accessing Internet if you are behind a firewall and/or proxy.

wget uses a config file wgetrc - and all proxy information is added in this file.

What messages do you get when conkywx updates?

This code has been same since ver 0.7.1 - so if it worked before - it should still do so. :)

Here are the .conkyrc changes...

```
<SNIP>


#####
## Load Lua for shading (optional)
## Set the path to your script here.
#
lua_load ~/.conky/draw_bg.lua
lua_draw_hook_pre draw_bg

####
## Load Lua for bargraphs (required)
## Set the path to your script here.
#
lua_load ~/.conky/bargraph_small.lua
lua_draw_hook_post main_bars

[B][COLOR="DarkRed"]####
## Define the locations of Conkywx files (required)
## Set the path to your .conf file & templates here.
#
####
## Define the locations of Conkywx files (required)
## Set the path to your .conf file & templates here.
#
template0 /home/vindsl/.conky/weather/conkywx.conf
template1 /home/vindsl/.conky/weather/vindsl-weather-template
template2 /home/vindsl/.conky/weather/vindsl-time-templatee[/COLOR][/B]

TEXT

<SNIP>

##################################
##    CONKYWX WEATHER SCRIPT    ##
##################################
${voffset 4}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8.25}${color4}CONKYWX${offset 8}${color6}${voffset -2}${hr 1}${font}
**[COLOR="DarkRed"]${execpi 900 conkywx -c ${template0} -t ${template1}|[/COLOR]**
####
## Windspeed [WS]
sed -e 's/Calm mph'/'\$\{offset 8\}Calm/g'|
####
## Moon Phases [MP]
sed -e 's/New Moon'/'\$\{offset -10\}New Moon/g'|
sed -e 's/Waxing Crescent'/'\$\{offset -3\}Waxing/g'|
sed -e 's/First Quarter'/'\$\{offset -3\}First Qtr/g'|
sed -e 's/Waxing Gibbous'/'\$\{offset -3\}Waxing/g'|
sed -e 's/Full Moon'/'\$\{offset -8\}Full Moon/g'|
sed -e 's/Waning Gibbous'/'\$\{offset -3\}Waning/g'|
sed -e 's/Last Quarter'/'\$\{offset -3\}Last Qtr/g'|
sed -e 's/Waning Crescent'/'\$\{offset -3\}Waning/g'
}${font}
##################################
##     CONKYWX TIME SCRIPT      ##
##################################
${voffset 6}${font DroidSans:bold:size=8}${color4}TIME${offset 8}${color6}${voffset -2}${hr 1}${font}
**[COLOR="DarkRed"]${execpi 900 conkywx -c ${template0} -t ${template2}}${font}[/COLOR]**

```

That knocked those lines down to size!  Works great, too!
